#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Google Verification - JWT SA and Payor OAuth access verification

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBGV_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgv multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBGV_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbgv_*)

zrbgv_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBGV_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgv already kindled"

  buc_log_args "Ensure dependencies are kindled first"
  zrbgc_sentinel
  zrbgo_sentinel
  zrbgu_sentinel
  zrbgp_sentinel

  # Kindle-constant temp file paths for forensic visibility
  readonly ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_resp.json"
  readonly ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_code.txt"
  readonly ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_stderr.txt"
  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_resp.json"
  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_code.txt"
  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_stderr.txt"

  readonly ZRBGV_KINDLED=1
}

zrbgv_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBGV_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbgv not kindled - call zrbgv_kindle first"
}

# Convert milliseconds to a decimal seconds string suitable for sleep
zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_ms="${1}"

  buc_log_args "Converting ${z_ms}ms to sleep duration"

  # Compute whole seconds and remainder milliseconds using integer arithmetic
  local z_sec=$(( z_ms / 1000 ))
  local z_rem=$(( z_ms % 1000 ))

  # Format as decimal with three fractional digits (sleep accepts e.g. 1.500)
  printf '%d.%03d' "${z_sec}" "${z_rem}"
}

# Resolve the RBRA file path for a given role name
# Role names: governor | director | retriever
zrbgv_role_rbra_file_capture() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_role="${1}"

  case "${z_role}" in
    governor)
      test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}"  || buc_die "RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE is not set"
      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}"
      ;;
    director)
      test -n "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE:-}"  || buc_die "RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE is not set"
      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}"
      ;;
    retriever)
      test -n "${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE:-}" || buc_die "RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE is not set"
      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE}"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown role '${z_role}': expected governor | director | retriever"
      ;;
  esac
}

# Execute one Artifact Registry packages.list probe iteration for a JWT SA role.
# Writes response to ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE, HTTP code to ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE,
# and stderr to ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE (kindle-constant paths for forensic visibility).
zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_role="${1}"
  local z_iteration="${2}"

  buc_log_args "JWT SA probe iteration ${z_iteration} for role '${z_role}'"

  buc_log_args "Resolve RBRA file for role"
  local z_rbra_file
  z_rbra_file=$(zrbgv_role_rbra_file_capture "${z_role}") || return 1

  buc_log_args "Validate RBRA file exists"
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "RBRA file not found for role ${z_role}: ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "Exchange JWT for OAuth token"
  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to obtain OAuth token for role ${z_role} (iteration ${z_iteration})"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Empty OAuth token for role ${z_role} (iteration ${z_iteration})"

  buc_log_args "Build Artifact Registry packages.list URL"
  test -n "${RBRR_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRR_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID is not set"
  test -n "${RBRR_GCP_REGION:-}"       || buc_die "RBRR_GCP_REGION is not set"
  test -n "${RBRR_GAR_REPOSITORY:-}"   || buc_die "RBRR_GAR_REPOSITORY is not set"

  local z_ar_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY}${RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1}/projects/${RBRR_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${RBRR_GCP_REGION}/repositories/${RBRR_GAR_REPOSITORY}/packages"

  buc_log_args "Call Artifact Registry packages.list"
  local z_curl_status=0
  curl                                              \
      -sS                                           \
      -X GET                                        \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}"         \
      -H "Accept: application/json"                 \
      -o "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}"                    \
      -w "%{http_code}"                             \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}"        \
      "${z_ar_url}" > "${ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE}"       \
                   2> "${ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE}"     \
    || z_curl_status=$?

  buc_log_args "AR curl exit status: ${z_curl_status}"
  buc_log_pipe < "${ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE}"

  test "${z_curl_status}" -eq 0 || buc_die "AR packages.list curl failed (network/SSL/DNS) for role ${z_role} iteration ${z_iteration}"

  local z_code
  z_code=$(<"${ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE}") || buc_die "Failed to read AR HTTP code file"
  test -n "${z_code}"                || buc_die "Empty HTTP code from AR curl"

  buc_log_args "AR packages.list HTTP ${z_code} for role ${z_role} iteration ${z_iteration}"

  case "${z_code}" in
    200|206)
      buc_step "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: OK (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    401|403)
      buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: access denied (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    *)
      local z_err=""
      if jq -e . "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null) || z_err="Unknown error"
      else
        z_err="Non-JSON response (HTTP ${z_code})"
      fi
      buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
      ;;
  esac
}

# Execute one CRM projects.get probe iteration for the Payor OAuth flow.
# Writes response to ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE, HTTP code to ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE,
# and stderr to ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE (kindle-constant paths for forensic visibility).
zrbgv_payor_crm_probe_once() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_iteration="${1}"

  buc_log_args "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}"

  buc_log_args "Authenticate via Payor OAuth refresh token flow"
  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) \
    || buc_die "Failed to obtain Payor OAuth access token (iteration ${z_iteration})"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Empty Payor OAuth access token (iteration ${z_iteration})"

  buc_log_args "Build CRM projects.get URL"
  test -n "${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID is not set"

  local z_crm_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V1}/projects/${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}"

  buc_log_args "Call CRM projects.get on payor project"
  local z_curl_status=0
  curl                                               \
      -sS                                            \
      -X GET                                         \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}"          \
      -H "Accept: application/json"                  \
      -o "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}"                    \
      -w "%{http_code}"                              \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}"         \
      "${z_crm_url}" > "${ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE}"      \
                    2> "${ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE}"    \
    || z_curl_status=$?

  buc_log_args "CRM curl exit status: ${z_curl_status}"
  buc_log_pipe < "${ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE}"

  test "${z_curl_status}" -eq 0 || buc_die "CRM projects.get curl failed (network/SSL/DNS) for Payor iteration ${z_iteration}"

  local z_code
  z_code=$(<"${ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE}") || buc_die "Failed to read CRM HTTP code file"
  test -n "${z_code}"                 || buc_die "Empty HTTP code from CRM curl"

  buc_log_args "CRM projects.get HTTP ${z_code} for Payor iteration ${z_iteration}"

  case "${z_code}" in
    200)
      buc_step "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: OK (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    401|403)
      buc_die "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: access denied (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    *)
      local z_err=""
      if jq -e . "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null) || z_err="Unknown error"
      else
        z_err="Non-JSON response (HTTP ${z_code})"
      fi
      buc_die "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
      ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbgv_*)

# Probe 1: JWT SA Access Probe
#
# For each iteration:
#   1. Exchange JWT for OAuth token via rbgo_get_token_capture with role's RBRA file
#   2. Call Artifact Registry packages.list to verify token grants read access
#   3. Sleep for configured delay
#
# All three roles have read access:
#   Governor  = Owner
#   Director  = repoAdmin
#   Retriever = reader
rbgv_jwt_sa_probe() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_role="${1:-}"
  local z_count="${2:-1}"
  local z_delay_ms="${3:-0}"

  buc_doc_brief "Probe JWT service account access for a given role against Artifact Registry"
  buc_doc_param "role"     "Role to probe: governor | director | retriever"
  buc_doc_param "count"    "Number of iterations (default: 1)"
  buc_doc_param "delay_ms" "Milliseconds to sleep between iterations (default: 0)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_role}"  || buc_die "role parameter required (governor | director | retriever)"
  test -n "${z_count}" || buc_die "count parameter required"

  buc_step "JWT SA access probe: role=${z_role} count=${z_count} delay_ms=${z_delay_ms}"

  local z_iter=1
  while test "${z_iter}" -le "${z_count}"; do
    buc_step "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iter}/${z_count}"

    zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once "${z_role}" "${z_iter}"

    if test "${z_iter}" -lt "${z_count}" && test "${z_delay_ms}" -gt 0; then
      local z_sleep
      z_sleep=$(zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture "${z_delay_ms}")
      buc_log_args "Sleeping ${z_sleep}s (${z_delay_ms}ms) before next iteration"
      sleep "${z_sleep}"
    fi

    z_iter=$(( z_iter + 1 ))
  done

  buc_step "JWT SA access probe complete: role=${z_role} iterations=${z_count}"
}

# Probe 2: Payor OAuth Access Probe
#
# For each iteration:
#   1. Authenticate via zrbgp_authenticate_capture (RBRO refresh token -> access token)
#   2. Call CRM projects.get on payor project to verify token works
#   3. Sleep for configured delay
rbgv_payor_oauth_probe() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_count="${1:-1}"
  local z_delay_ms="${2:-0}"

  buc_doc_brief "Probe Payor OAuth access against Cloud Resource Manager (CRM) projects.get"
  buc_doc_param "count"    "Number of iterations (default: 1)"
  buc_doc_param "delay_ms" "Milliseconds to sleep between iterations (default: 0)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_count}" || buc_die "count parameter required"

  buc_step "Payor OAuth access probe: count=${z_count} delay_ms=${z_delay_ms}"

  local z_iter=1
  while test "${z_iter}" -le "${z_count}"; do
    buc_step "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iter}/${z_count}"

    zrbgv_payor_crm_probe_once "${z_iter}"

    if test "${z_iter}" -lt "${z_count}" && test "${z_delay_ms}" -gt 0; then
      local z_sleep
      z_sleep=$(zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture "${z_delay_ms}")
      buc_log_args "Sleeping ${z_sleep}s (${z_delay_ms}ms) before next iteration"
      sleep "${z_sleep}"
    fi

    z_iter=$(( z_iter + 1 ))
  done

  buc_step "Payor OAuth access probe complete: iterations=${z_count}"
}

# eof

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation probes
5	{gar_service}
6	read access for a specified
7	{rbtr_role}
8	by performing a JWT-to-OAuth token exchange and then calling the Artifact Registry packages.list API.
9	It may repeat for a configured number of iterations with a configurable delay, enabling sustained access verification and token lifecycle testing.
10	
11	The operation accepts a role parameter selecting among
12	{rbtr_governor},
13	{rbtr_director},
14	and
15	{rbtr_retriever},
16	each with Owner, repoAdmin, and reader permissions respectively.
17	All three roles have Artifact Registry read access, so a successful response confirms both token validity and role-specific permission.
18	
19	//axhos_step
20	. *Validate Input Parameters*:
21	.. {rbbc_require}
22	`آ«INPUT_ROLEآ»` provided as first argument: one of `governor`, `director`, or `retriever`
23	.. {rbbc_fatal}
24	if role missing: "Role parameter required (governor|director|retriever)"
25	.. {rbbc_fatal}
26	if role unrecognized: "Unknown role: آ«INPUT_ROLEآ» â€” must be governor, director, or retriever"
27	.. {rbbc_store}
28	`آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»` from `--count` flag (default: 1)
29	.. {rbbc_store}
30	`آ«DELAY_MSآ»` from `--delay_ms` flag (default: 0)
31	.. {rbbc_fatal}
32	if `آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»` is not a positive integer: "Count must be a positive integer"
33	.. {rbbc_fatal}
34	if `آ«DELAY_MSآ»` is not a non-negative integer: "Delay must be a non-negative integer"
35	
36	//axhos_step
37	. *Resolve Role Configuration*:
38	.. If `آ«INPUT_ROLEآ»` is `governor`:
39	... {rbbc_store}
40	`آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ»` as `Governor`
41	... {rbbc_store}
42	`آ«RBRA_FILEآ»` from
43	{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}
44	.. If `آ«INPUT_ROLEآ»` is `director`:
45	... {rbbc_store}
46	`آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ»` as `Director`
47	... {rbbc_store}
48	`آ«RBRA_FILEآ»` from
49	{rbrr_director_rbra_file}
50	.. If `آ«INPUT_ROLEآ»` is `retriever`:
51	... {rbbc_store}
52	`آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ»` as `Retriever`
53	... {rbbc_store}
54	`آ«RBRA_FILEآ»` from
55	{rbrr_retriever_rbra_file}
56	.. {rbbc_require}
57	`آ«RBRA_FILEآ»` exists on the filesystem
58	.. {rbbc_fatal}
59	if file missing: "آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» RBRA file missing at آ«RBRA_FILEآ»"
60	
61	//axhos_step
62	. *Construct Verification Endpoint*:
63	.. {rbbc_store}
64	`آ«VERIFY_URLآ»` as:
65	+
66	`https://artifactregistry.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{rbrr_depot_project_id}/locations/{rbrr_gcp_region}/repositories/{rbrr_gar_repository}/packages`
67	
68	//axhos_step
69	. *Execute Probe Iterations*:
70	.. For each iteration `آ«Iآ»` from 1 to `آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»`:
71	
72	... *Acquire آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» Token*:
73	.... Execute
74	{rbtoe_jwt_oauth_exchange}
75	with `آ«RBRA_FILEآ»`
76	.... {rbbc_store}
77	`آ«ROLE_TOKENآ»` from exchange result
78	.... {rbbc_fatal}
79	if token exchange fails: "Iteration آ«Iآ»: JWT-to-OAuth exchange failed for آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ»"
80	
81	... *Call Artifact Registry Packages API*:
82	.... {rbbc_call}
83	link:https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories.packages/list[artifactregistry.projects.locations.repositories.packages.list] with:
84	..... method: `GET`
85	..... endpoint: `آ«VERIFY_URLآ»`
86	..... header: `Authorization: Bearer آ«ROLE_TOKENآ»`
87	.... {rbbc_store}
88	`آ«HTTP_STATUSآ»` from response status code
89	.... {rbbc_store}
90	`آ«RESPONSE_BODYآ»` from response body
91	
92	... *Evaluate Response*:
93	.... If `آ«HTTP_STATUSآ»` is 200:
94	..... {rbbc_show}
95	`Iteration آ«Iآ»/آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»: آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» OK â€” packages.list HTTP 200`
96	.... If `آ«HTTP_STATUSآ»` is 403:
97	..... {rbbc_fatal}
98	"Iteration آ«Iآ»: آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» access denied â€” HTTP 403 (token valid but role lacks Artifact Registry read permission)"
99	.... If `آ«HTTP_STATUSآ»` is 401:
100	..... {rbbc_fatal}
101	"Iteration آ«Iآ»: آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» token rejected â€” HTTP 401 (token exchange succeeded but token is invalid)"
102	.... Otherwise:
103	..... {rbbc_fatal}
104	"Iteration آ«Iآ»: آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» unexpected HTTP آ«HTTP_STATUSآ»: آ«RESPONSE_BODYآ»"
105	
106	... *Apply Inter-Iteration Delay* (if not final iteration):
107	.... If `آ«DELAY_MSآ»` is greater than 0 and `آ«Iآ»` is less than `آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»`:
108	..... {rbbc_show}
109	`Sleeping آ«DELAY_MSآ»ms before next iteration`
110	..... Sleep `آ«DELAY_MSآ»` milliseconds
111	
112	//axhos_step
113	. *Display Summary*:
114	.. {rbbc_show}
115	`آ«ROLE_DISPLAYآ» JWT access probe complete: آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ»/آ«ITERATION_COUNTآ» iterations passed`
116	
117	//axhoc_completion
118	Procedure completes successfully when all iterations confirm that the selected role's JWT-derived OAuth token grants read access to the
119	{gar_service}
120	packages list endpoint.
121	Completion demonstrates both that JWT-to-OAuth token exchange is operational for the role and that the resulting token carries the expected Artifact Registry read permissions.
122

1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system â€” every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction â€” must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" â€” load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing â€” extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation â€” same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** â€” the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	آ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	آ«prefixآ»_cli.sh (executable entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code â†’ CLI script â†’ furnish() kindles â†’ module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# â‌Œ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# âœ… External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
85	
86	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
87	
88	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
89	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
90	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
91	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
92	
93	### Regime Module Archetype
94	
95	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields â€” the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
96	
97	| Component | Location | Purpose |
98	|-----------|----------|---------|
99	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
100	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
101	| `zآ«prefixآ»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
102	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
103	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` â€” explained per-field display |
104	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` â€” diagnostic dump |
105	
106	Furnish sequence: kindle â†’ enforce â†’ lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
107	
108	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
109	
110	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
111	
112	---
113	
114	## Function Patterns
115	
116	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
117	
118	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
119	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
120	| `zآ«prefixآ»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
121	| `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
122	| `zآ«prefixآ»_enforce`  | Implementation | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
123	| `zآ«prefixآ»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
124	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
125	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
126	
127	**Kindle constant**: Any variable â€” internal (`Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`آ«PREFIXآ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) â€” defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function â€” including enroll, setup, or helper functions â€” may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
128	
129	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_آ«prefixآ»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
130	
131	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`آ«PREFIXآ»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must be pure string literals with **no variable expansion, no computation, and no runtime dependency**. They are available immediately after sourcing â€” no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling â€” inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
132	
133	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
134	
135	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
136	
137	This table defines scope: آ«prefixآ»_* is public, zآ«prefixآ»_* is internal.
138	
139	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
140	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
141	| Public          | `آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»` | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
142	| Internal helper | `zآ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ»`   | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
143	
144	---
145	
146	## File Templates
147	
148	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
149	
150	```bash
151	#!/bin/bash
152	#
153	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
154	#
155	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
156	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
157	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
158	#
159	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
160	#
161	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
162	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
163	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
164	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
165	# limitations under the License.
166	#
167	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
168	#
169	```
170	
171	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
172	
173	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
174	
175	| Variable | Source | Value |
176	|----------|--------|-------|
177	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
178	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
179	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
180	
181	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
182	
183	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
184	
185	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars â€” furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
186	
187	```bash
188	# آ«shebangآ»
189	# آ«copyrightآ»
190	
191	set -euo pipefail
192	
193	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
194	
195	zآ«prefixآ»_furnish() {
196	
197	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
198	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
199	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
200	  buc_doc_env "آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
201	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
202	
203	  # Source all dependencies
204	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
205	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ».sh"
206	
207	  # Source regime files, if any
208	  source "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
209	
210	  zآ«prefixآ»_kindle
211	  zآ«prefixآ»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
212	}
213	
214	buc_execute آ«prefixآ»_ "آ«module_description_phraseآ»" zآ«prefixآ»_furnish "$@"
215	
216	# eof
217	```
218	
219	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
220	
221	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
222	
223	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
224	
225	```bash
226	zآ«prefixآ»_furnish() {
227	  local z_command="${1:-}"
228	
229	  # Document environment variables
230	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
231	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
232	  buc_doc_env "آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
233	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
234	
235	  # Always source light dependencies
236	  source "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
237	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
238	
239	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
240	  case "${z_command}" in
241	    آ«prefixآ»_survey|آ«prefixآ»_audit)
242	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
243	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
244	      zheavy_kindle
245	      ;;
246	    آ«prefixآ»_validate|آ«prefixآ»_render)
247	      # These commands work with config only
248	      ;;
249	  esac
250	
251	  zآ«prefixآ»_kindle
252	}
253	```
254	
255	**Key points:**
256	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "آ«prefixآ»_validate") as `$1`
257	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
258	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
259	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
260	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
261	
262	**Benefits:**
263	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
264	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
265	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
266	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
267	
268	### Template 3: Implementation Module
269	
270	```bash
271	# آ«shebangآ»
272	# آ«copyrightآ»
273	
274	set -euo pipefail
275	
276	# Multiple inclusion detection
277	test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
278	Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED=1
279	
280	# Tinder constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion â€” available at source time)
281	# آ«PREFIXآ»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
282	# آ«PREFIXآ»_some_prefix="prefix_"
283	
284	######################################################################
285	# Internal Functions (zآ«prefixآ»_*)
286	
287	zآ«prefixآ»_kindle() {
288	  test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» already kindled"
289	
290	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
291	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
292	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
293	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
294	
295	  # Validate module specific environment
296	  buv_file_exists "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
297	
298	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
299	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
300	
301	  # Kindle constant (public â€” safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
302	  آ«PREFIXآ»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
303	
304	  # Kindle constants (internal)
305	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
306	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_result.json"
307	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
308	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
309	
310	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
311	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
312	
313	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1
314	}
315	
316	zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel() {
317	  test "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» not kindled - call zآ«prefixآ»_kindle first"
318	}
319	
320	# Helper functions follow
321	
322	######################################################################
323	# External Functions (آ«prefixآ»_*)
324	
325	آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»() {
326	  zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel
327	
328	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
329	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
330	
331	  # Documentation block
332	  buc_doc_brief "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
333	  buc_doc_param "آ«required_argآ»" "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
334	  buc_doc_oparm "آ«optional_argآ»" "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
335	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
336	
337	  # Implementation
338	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
339	
340	  buc_step "آ«user_visible_milestoneآ»"
341	  buc_log_args "آ«detailed_progress_informationآ»"
342	  [command] || buc_die "آ«failure_explanationآ»"
343	
344	  buc_success "آ«success_messageآ»"
345	}
346	```
347	
348	**Kindle constant â€” anti-pattern:**
349	
350	```bash
351	# â‌Œ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
352	zآ«prefixآ»_setup() {
353	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
354	}
355	
356	# âœ… Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
357	zآ«prefixآ»_kindle() {
358	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
359	  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1
360	}
361	```
362	
363	**Tinder vs kindle constant â€” choosing correctly:**
364	
365	```bash
366	# âœ… Tinder constant: pure string, no variable expansion, available at source time
367	آ«PREFIXآ»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name â€” no kindle needed
368	
369	# âœ… Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
370	آ«PREFIXآ»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name â€” kindle required
371	
372	# â‌Œ Wrong: variable expansion in tinder constant position
373	آ«PREFIXآ»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: uses ${}, must be in kindle
374	
375	# â‌Œ Wrong: computation in tinder constant position
376	آ«PREFIXآ»_color_red=$(zآ«prefixآ»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() â€” must be in kindle
377	```
378	
379	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
380	
381	### Expansion Requirements
382	
383	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
384	```bash
385	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion

389	
390	**Never use:**
391	```bash
392	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
393	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
394	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
395	```

397	**No tilde in quoted strings** â€” tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
398	```bash

file.content:
<!--
Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
See LICENSE for terms.
-->

# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference

## Core Philosophy

This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.

The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.

Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.

Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.

The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.

### Load-Bearing Complexity

Every element in a system â€” every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction â€” must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.

This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" â€” load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"

Concrete examples from this codebase:

- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing â€” extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation â€” same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** â€” the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.

Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.

## Module Architecture

Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.

```bash
آ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
آ«prefixآ»_cli.sh (executable entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
```

### CLI as Module Gateway

**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.

**Pattern**:
```
External Code â†’ CLI script â†’ furnish() kindles â†’ module functions
```

**Why this matters**:
- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality

**Anti-pattern**:
```bash
# â‌Œ External script kindling directly
source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
rbob_charge
```

**Correct pattern**:
```bash
# âœ… External script delegates to CLI
exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
```

**What counts as "external code"**:
- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
- TabTargets and launchers
- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules

**What can call kindle**:
- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior

### Module-CLI Prefix Unity

**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).

When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
dependencies based on which command is being invoked.

### Regime Module Archetype

A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields â€” the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:

| Component | Location | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
| `zآ«prefixآ»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` â€” explained per-field display |
| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` â€” diagnostic dump |

Furnish sequence: kindle â†’ enforce â†’ lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.

**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.

Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.

---

## Function Patterns

### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)

| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `zآ«prefixآ»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
| `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
| `zآ«prefixآ»_enforce`  | Implementation | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
| `zآ«prefixآ»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |

**Kindle constant**: Any variable â€” internal (`Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`آ«PREFIXآ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) â€” defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function â€” including enroll, setup, or helper functions â€” may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.

**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_آ«prefixآ»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.

**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`آ«PREFIXآ»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must be pure string literals with **no variable expansion, no computation, and no runtime dependency**. They are available immediately after sourcing â€” no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling â€” inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.

**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.

### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)

This table defines scope: آ«prefixآ»_* is public, zآ«prefixآ»_* is internal.

| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
| Public          | `آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»` | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
| Internal helper | `zآ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ»`   | `zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |

---

## File Templates

### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
```

### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables

Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():

| Variable | Source | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |

These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.

### Template 2: CLI Entry Point

BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars â€” furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.

```bash
# آ«shebangآ»
# آ«copyrightآ»

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

zآ«prefixآ»_furnish() {

  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Source all dependencies
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«nameآ».sh"

  # Source regime files, if any
  source "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"

  zآ«prefixآ»_kindle
  zآ«prefixآ»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
}

buc_execute آ«prefixآ»_ "آ«module_description_phraseآ»" zآ«prefixآ»_furnish "$@"

# eof
```

### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)

The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.

**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.

```bash
zآ«prefixآ»_furnish() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"

  # Document environment variables
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Always source light dependencies
  source "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"

  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
  case "${z_command}" in
    آ«prefixآ»_survey|آ«prefixآ»_audit)
      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
      zheavy_kindle
      ;;
    آ«prefixآ»_validate|آ«prefixآ»_render)
      # These commands work with config only
      ;;
  esac

  zآ«prefixآ»_kindle
}
```

**Key points:**
- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "آ«prefixآ»_validate") as `$1`
- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies

**Benefits:**
- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure

### Template 3: Implementation Module

```bash
# آ«shebangآ»
# آ«copyrightآ»

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
Zآ«PREFIXآ»_SOURCED=1

# Tinder constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion â€” available at source time)
# آ«PREFIXآ»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
# آ«PREFIXآ»_some_prefix="prefix_"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zآ«prefixآ»_*)

zآ«prefixآ»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» already kindled"

  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps

  # Validate module specific environment
  buv_file_exists "${آ«PREFIXآ»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present

  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"

  # Kindle constant (public â€” safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
  آ«PREFIXآ»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"

  # Kindle constants (internal)
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_result.json"
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»_آ«step_numberآ»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use

  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"

  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1
}

zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel() {
  test "${Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module آ«prefixآ» not kindled - call zآ«prefixآ»_kindle first"
}

# Helper functions follow

######################################################################
# External Functions (آ«prefixآ»_*)

آ«prefixآ»_آ«commandآ»() {
  zآ«prefixآ»_sentinel

  local z_param1="${1:-}"
  local z_param2="${2:-}"

  # Documentation block
  buc_doc_brief "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
  buc_doc_param "آ«required_argآ»" "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
  buc_doc_oparm "آ«optional_argآ»" "آ«one_line_descriptionآ»"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Implementation
  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"

  buc_step "آ«user_visible_milestoneآ»"
  buc_log_args "آ«detailed_progress_informationآ»"
  [command] || buc_die "آ«failure_explanationآ»"

  buc_success "آ«success_messageآ»"
}
```

**Kindle constant â€” anti-pattern:**

```bash
# â‌Œ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
zآ«prefixآ»_setup() {
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
}

# âœ… Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
zآ«prefixآ»_kindle() {
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
  Zآ«PREFIXآ»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Tinder vs kindle constant â€” choosing correctly:**

```bash
# âœ… Tinder constant: pure string, no variable expansion, available at source time
آ«PREFIXآ»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name â€” no kindle needed

# âœ… Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
آ«PREFIXآ»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name â€” kindle required

# â‌Œ Wrong: variable expansion in tinder constant position
آ«PREFIXآ»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: uses ${}, must be in kindle

# â‌Œ Wrong: computation in tinder constant position
آ«PREFIXآ»_color_red=$(zآ«prefixآ»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() â€” must be in kindle
```

## Variable Handling (General Rules)

### Expansion Requirements

**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
```bash
"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
```

**Never use:**
```bash
$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
```

**No tilde in quoted strings** â€” tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
```bash
# â‌Œ Tilde does not expand in quotes
"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory

1201	### â‌Œ Inline Shellcheck Directives
1202	
1203	```bash
1204	# â‌Œ Never suppress shellcheck inline
1205	# shellcheck disable=SC2086
1206	some_command ${z_unquoted}
1207

1212	
1213	All BCG-structural shellcheck suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. No inline `# shellcheck` directives of any kind. If a shellcheck code fires and it's BCG-structural, add it to `busc_shellcheckrc` with rationale. If it's a genuine finding, fix the code.
1214	
1215	### â‌Œ Commit-Message Comments
1216	
1217	A **commit-message comment** describes the *change event* rather than the *code*. It narrates what was added, when, or why it was inserted â€” information that belongs in `git log`, not in the file. The tell: the comment would make more sense as a commit summary than as documentation for a future reader.
1218	
1219	```bash
1220	# â‌Œ Commit-message comment: narrates the insertion
1221	# Added qualification subsystem for shellcheck support
1222	buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;
1223	
1224	# â‌Œ Commit-message comment: documents the change, not the code
1225	# New helper for retry logic (see PR #142)
1226	zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {
1227	
1228	# âœ… No comment needed â€” the code is self-evident
1229	buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;
1230	
1231	# âœ… Comment describes the code, not the change
1232	# Retry with exponential backoff; max delay capped at 60s
1233	zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {
1234	```
1235	
1236	**Litmus test**: Would this comment be better as a commit message? If yes, delete it from the code â€” it's already captured in git history.
1237	
1238	This is a characteristic LLM failure mode. Models narrate their own work process into code, writing for the person reviewing the diff rather than the person reading the file next year. The resulting comments assume a supervisory reader with conversation context â€” an audience that does not exist at maintenance time.
1239	
1240	---
1241	
1242	## Interface Contamination Discipline
1243	
1244	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
1245	
1246	### Zeroes Theory
1247	
1248	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.
1249	
1250	### Rules
1251	
1252	1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
1253	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.
1254	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `${1:-default}` patterns that silently fill missing required arguments. Required means required.
1255	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input. If input doesn't match the canonical form, `buc_die` with a clear message stating the expected form.
1256	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation â€” never a tolerated alternative.
1257	
1258	### Smell Test
1259	
1260	If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.
1261	
1262	---
1263	
1264	## `set -e` is Not Sufficient
1265	
1266	**The POSIX suppression rule**: `set -e` is suppressed inside `if`, `while`, `||`, `&&` test expressions, and this propagates through the **entire call tree** of the tested command.
1267	
1268	```bash
1269	# â‌Œ set -e suppressed for entire call tree of some_function
1270	if some_function; then ...
1271	
1272	# Even if some_function calls other_function that fails,
1273	# set -e will not terminate â€” suppression propagates
1274	```
1275	
1276	**BCG rule: Only `_predicate` functions may appear in `if`/`while` conditions.** All other functions must be invoked as simple commands with explicit `|| buc_die` / `|| return`. This completely prevents the suppression hazard.
1277	
1278	```bash
1279	# âœ… Predicate in conditional â€” designed for this, never dies, status only
1280	if zآ«prefixآ»_ready_predicate; then ...
1281	
1282	# âœ… Regular function â€” explicit error handling, not inside conditional
1283	some_function || buc_die "..."
1284	
1285	# â‌Œ Regular function in conditional â€” set -e suppressed for entire call tree
1286	if some_function; then ...
1287	```
1288	
1289	**Why BCG's `|| buc_die` discipline works**: It doesn't rely on `set -e` â€” explicit error handling after every command means suppression doesn't matter. The `|| z_status=$?` capture pattern intentionally relies on this suppression behavior.
1290	
1291	**Loops**: `set -e` does not reliably catch failures inside `for`/`while` loop bodies in bash 3.2. Every command in a loop body must have explicit error handling.
1292	
1293	The error handling suffix depends on the function type:
1294	
1295	| Function type    | On failure use       | Why                                             |
1296	|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
1297	| Regular/enroll   | `\|\| buc_die`       | Enroll validates invariants; violations are fatal |
1298	| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
1299	| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
1300	| Yawp             | N/A                  | Cannot fail â€” pure builtins only                 |
1301	| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |
1302	
1303	### â‌Œ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`
1304	
1305	```bash
1306	# â‌Œ Relies on set -e exemption for &&/|| lists â€” fragile
1307	test "${z_val}" = "done" && break
1308	test -z "${z_val}" && return 0
1309	[[ "${z_line}" == *"pattern"* ]] && continue
1310	
1311	# âœ… Invert the test, use || â€” same behavior, no exemption reliance
1312	test "${z_val}" != "done" || break
1313	test -n "${z_val}" || return 0
1314	[[ "${z_line}" != *"pattern"* ]] || continue
1315	```
1316	
1317	**BCG rule: Never `test ... && break/continue/return`.** Always invert the test and use `||`. The `||` form is already the BCG standard for control flow after a test; the `&&` form relies on humans remembering that bash exempts `&&`/`||` lists from `set -e`, which is a language-lawyer trap.
1318	
1319	### â‌Œ `A && B || C` as Pseudo-Ternary
1320	
1321	```bash
1322	# â‌Œ C runs both when A fails AND when B fails â€” not equivalent to if/then/else
1323	curl "${z_url}" && jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed"
1324	
1325	# âœ… Explicit if/then/else â€” C runs only when A fails
1326	if curl "${z_url}"; then
1327	  jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "jq failed"
1328	else
1329	  buc_die "curl failed"
1330	fi
1331	```
1332	
1333	**BCG rule: Never `A && B || C`.** This is not if-then-else. When `A` succeeds but `B` fails, `C` runs â€” which is almost never the intended behavior. Use explicit `if`/`then`/`else` for conditional execution with a fallback.
1334	
1335	---
1336	
1337	## Array Safety Under `set -u`
1338	
1339	Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.
1340	
1341	**Index iteration** â€” use when you need the index (parallel arrays, kindle rolls). Inherently safe on empty arrays, no guard needed:
1342	
1343	```bash
1344	for z_i in "${!z_آ«prefixآ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1345	  echo "${z_آ«prefixآ»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1346	done
1347	```
1348	
1349	**Guarded value iteration** â€” use when you only need values. The `(( ))` guard prevents expansion of the empty array:
1350	
1351	```bash
1352	if (( ${#z_آ«prefixآ»_name_roll[@]} )); then
1353	  for z_val in "${z_آ«prefixآ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1354	    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1355	  done
1356	fi
1357	```
1358	
1359	**Anti-pattern** â€” unguarded value iteration on a possibly-empty array:
1360	
1361	```bash
1362	# â‌Œ Crashes under set -u in bash 3.2 when array is empty
1363	for z_val in "${z_آ«prefixآ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1364	  echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1365	done
1366	```
1367	
1368	---
